data.title: Power Business To Drive Solaris Energy As Oilfield Activity Softens (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Data Center Demand Fuels Growth

I discussed Solaris Oilfield Infrastructure (NYSE:SEI) and you can read the previous article here, published on September 4, 2024. SEI’s logistics segment is under pressure as oil price weakness cuts drilling and completion activity. Utilization is falling, fixed costs are heavy, and segment EBITDA is sliding. Power Solutions is the growth driver, helped by rising demand from AI, electrification, and grid resiliency.

The company is scaling fast with contracts, acquisitions, and the Stateline JV boosting capacity and visibility. While the climb in revenue and EBITDA is commendable, capex is depleting cash flow, and debt is moving up. The stock appears reasonably valued compared to its peers. Even with leverage risk, I think its strategies and drivers are robust enough to assign a "Buy" call on the stock.

Why Do I Upgrade My Rating?

In my previous discussion on SEI, published in September 2024, when I recommended a “Hold,” I wrote:

The upcoming MER acquisition is pivotal for SEI, given the sticky water it has recently found itself in. By incorporating a mobile distributed power market, it aims to add a revenue stream that can increase revenue per frac crew. MER’s impressive investment plan can generate higher demand growth across multiple end markets. MER plans to more than triple its fleet size capacity by 2025. As the energy market outlook deteriorated, sales decreased, translating into lower cash flow from operations. In connection with the MER Acquisition, SEI secured a $300 million loan and expects to obtain alternative financing.

After Q2, SEI’s logistics business is weakening with lower utilization and margin pressure. Power Solutions is scaling quickly with higher demand from data centres and digital technology. Revenue and EBITDA are rising, but high capex and debt remain key risks. Nonetheless, given the upside, I upgrade my rating to a “Buy.”

Acquisition Benefits Mobile Power Business

As I discussed in my previous article, in July 2024, SEI had announced the acquisition of Mobile Energy Rentals. The acquisition brought in a new mobile distributed power product line to Solaris’s offerings. MER’s power generation asset was expected to grow to 478 MW by Q3 2025 (from 153 MW) following the purchase of additional mobile turbines. Modular generation lets customers scale power gradually, matching capacity with actual data center demand. Investors may note that AI-driven workloads don’t need peak power from day one. At the same time, new regulations (Senate Bill 6 in Texas), can encourage co-located generation. The policy change can drive fresh commercial opportunities and enhance the value of distributed solutions.

Mobile power supply is evolving into a significant market. According to a Goldman Sachs report (as cited by the company), by 2027, AI server racks could use 50x more power than older cloud racks, with tech roadmaps already pointing to 1 megawatt per rack. SEI’s power solutions use turbines and reciprocating engines to cut time to power, lower emissions, and boost reliability. That way, it mixes technologies to match each project’s needs. Recently, it made SCR systems (Selective Catalytic Reduction systems) more mobile, which should speed up setup and reduce downtime on site.

Power Business and Strategies

SEI is scaling its power business quickly, growing from 150 megawatts in 2024 to over 600 megawatts across six customers. I expect contracts to drive further capacity additions in the coming quarters. End markets are broadening into microgrids, gas processing, utility grid resiliency, and AI data centers.

Demand is accelerating as electrification, AI, and reshoring trends strain the grid. The company’s strategy centers on modular, power-dense systems with hybrid microgrid structures that enhance redundancy and reliability. I think the company’s modular, reliable, and lower-emission solutions provide the company a competitive edge.

Recent Initiatives

Solaris has formed Stateline Power, a joint venture with a data center customer, to co-own and operate about 900 megawatts at one site. The JV will add $62 million in EBITDA to Solaris shareholders, which will give investors a clearer view of true profitability. In August 2025, it acquired HVMVLV to broaden its Power Solutions offering. The acquisition added electrical equipment, engineering, and design services to SEI’s portfolio.

Completion Activity Challenges and Risks

In Q2, SEI’s fully utilized systems (including sand systems and top fill systems) declined. It averaged 94 active systems in Q2, down 4%. The company expects softer crude oil prices to reduce drilling and completion activity. It can lower utilization by 10–15% in Q3. Because its Logistics segment involves high fixed costs, the segment margin can come under pressure, leading to a sharp EBITDA decline for the segment. I think the Power Solutions segment’s EBITDA growth will offset the weaker EBITDA of the Logistics segment. So, the company’s EBITDA can stay flat at $58 million–$63 million.

Over the years, Solaris has scaled from one system earning $1 million per frac crew to four systems generating about $4 million per crew. The completion industry trends point to fewer active crews needed to sustain production. Solaris’s sand system equipment should support the efficiency gains and higher productivity per crew.

I see low natural gas prices and crude oil supply exceeding demand weighing on the energy market’s outlook. As a result, its frac crews have been downsized. According to Primary Vision data, frac spread count decreased by 18% year-to-date. Rig count, during this period, declined by 9%. Lower completion activity and frac spread count can adversely affect SEI’s performance in Q3.

My Estimates

Over the past ten quarters, its adjusted EBITDA increased by 17% on average. Given the impact of the drop in frac crews, offset by higher margin from power business, I expect the topline and EBITDA growth to accelerate moderately. Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 15%-20%.

Analyzing The Q2 Performance

As disclosed in the Q2 2025 earnings press release dated July 23, from Q1 to Q2, SEI’s revenues increased by 18%. Strong growth in Power Solutions primarily led the rise in revenues. Adjusted EBITDA increased by 29% from Q1 to Q2. Lower fully utilized systems and an increase in lower-margin ancillary last-mile logistics services activity contributed to decreased EBITDA margin.

Cash Flows And Balance Sheet

In 6M 2025, SE’s cash flow from operations increased by 40% compared to a year ago. Higher revenues in the past year primarily led to the rise. Despite that, its free cash flows deteriorated and turned negative due to the capex on turbines and ancillary equipment to support the growth and operations.

SEI's liquidity was $195 million as of June 30, 2025. Its leverage (debt-to-equity) 0.5x was higher than its peers' average (PUMP, WTTR, and LBRT). As of Q2 2025, total debt stood at about $552 million. Its debt could rise to nearly $1 billion by 2027, though about a quarter of that will be borne by its JV partner.

Analyst Rating

According to Seeking Alpha, nine analysts rated it a "Buy " (including “Strong Buy”). None of the analysts rated it a “Hold” or a “sell." The consensus target price is $45.1, suggesting 49% upside at the current price.

Relative Valuation And My Target Price

SEI's forward EV/EBITDA multiple contraction versus the current EV/EBITDA is much steeper than its peers' average (PUMP, WTTR, and LBRT). This implies its EBITDA is expected to rise more sharply than its peers next year. This typically results in a much higher EV/EBITDA multiple. The company's EV/EBITDA multiple (13.9x) is higher than its peers' average of 3.8x. So, I think the stock is reasonably valued versus its peers at this level.

SEI's five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple is 12.8x. If it trades at that multiple, the stock price can rise by 49% the current level. Since my last publication in September 2024, when I suggested a "Hold," the stock has increased by approximately 157%.

As I discussed earlier in the article, I expect 15%- 20% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters. Feeding these values into the EV calculation and applying the current sell-side EV/EBITDA multiple of ~14x, I think the stock should trade between $59 and $61, implying a ~98% upside.

What's The Take On SEI?

SEI’s Logistics business is under pressure as oil price softness reduces drilling and completion activity. Lower utilization and high fixed costs are weighing on margins, though Power Solutions growth is helping to balance EBITDA. Industry activity has slowed with fewer frac crews and rigs, but SEI’s equipment supports efficiency gains and higher productivity per crew. Near term, I see weakness in completions, but long-term demand for mobile power remains strong.

The power business is scaling rapidly, with contracts expanding capacity and demand rising from AI, electrification, and grid needs. Recent acquisitions and the Stateline JV broaden its technical capabilities and earnings visibility. Revenue and EBITDA rose sharply in Q2, but heavy capex pushed free cash flow negative. Its debt level has also mounted as capex on turbines and ancillary equipment increased. Despite the short-term hiccups, the Stateline Power JV and the HVMVLV acquisition can boost the company’s foray into the mobile power business and secure its long-term growth potential. I suggest investors “Buy” it with strong medium-term returns.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Duluth Has Changed Strategy And Is Working, But The Stock Is Too Optimistic
data.description:
Duluth Trading (NASDAQ:DLTH) reported positive Q2'25 results, the first after replacing its CEO in early Q2'25.

The results and the call provided important information regarding the company's strategy post-CEO change. I believe the changes, especially those related to assortment and digital strategy, are very positive.

Overall, I maintain the view that I have had on Duluth for some time already: the name is cheap compared to historical standards because execution has been very frail and because the company's cash position, while not dramatic, is not great. I believe this does not change in one quarter, and I maintain a Hold on DLTH.

A peek into a potentially successful strategy

I believe the company's results surprised the market, with the stock climbing almost 100% on the day after the results, because they show a glimpse of what could be possible under better execution. The company posted operating and net profitability, something that had not occurred in a second quarter for years. Most importantly, the company gained profits while losing sales.

Sheding unprofitable DTC

At the core of the new strategy is the abandonment of unprofitable sales, a topic I have discussed at large with ailing retailers like DLTH. Management teams sometimes decide that topline growth is the most important metric and therefore promote sales at the expense of profitability via enormous discounting and even larger expenses on online traffic acquisition. This 'empty calories' sales strategy is very common in DTC-heavy retailers, like DLTH itself.

DLTH has decided that it will cut unprofitable sales, even if that implies the topline declines. We saw that in this quarter, aggregate sales were down 7%, with DTC down 14% and stores up 5% (DTC is about 2/3 of the company's sales). The decrease, according to the call, occurred because traffic to online stores decreased due to lower promotions.

The first sign that the strategy might work is that sales declined 7%, but gross profits 'only' 2.3%, because gross margins expanded by almost 250 bps. That is, for each $1 of sales it lost, the company only lost $0.35 in gross profits. These were sales that were lower in gross profitability (30/35%) than the company's aggregate (~50/55%).

The next sign of success was seen in SG&A decreasing by $5 million while gross profits decreased by $2 million. The decrease in SG&A, according to the release, was due to lower outbound shipping costs (DTC shipping).

That is, the company lost $2 million in gross profits, for which it was spending about $5 million in shipping and SG&A. The net result is an improvement of $2 million in adjusted EBITDA and a flip from breakeven in Q2'24 to $3 million in operating income in Q2'25 (adding back restructuring in both cases).

During the call, the new CEO announced that the team is looking at gross profit dollars as the metric to follow. I believe this is correct, in that gross profits are more important than sales alone, but I would like to see a focus on operating income rather than gross profits.

Reducing SKUs

In previous articles on DLTH, I was always critical of the company's impressive SKU inflation. The company launched many varieties of the same products and was constantly introducing new categories, some of which were fairly disconnected from its core competencies (apparel for the most part).

Positively, this quarter, the company announced that it expects 2026 collections to reduce the SKU count by 20%. Lower SKUs imply lower assortment expenses, better volume purchase discounts, and lower need to over-promote in order to clear slow-moving inventory.

Lessons from the past

The company's new CEO had been in charge of the company between 2015 and 2019. The period is illustrative of some challenges with retailing. During those years, Duluth grew revenues and gross profits because it was expanding its store base and gaining clients online. However, the cost was higher SG&A, which ate literally all of the gains in gross profitability. The result was a company that was larger but no more profitable. I hope this time management can learn from the past and concentrate on actual profitability.

Valuation is speculative given uncertainty

We have seen several retail turnaround stories in the past year and a half. They all showed improvements in profitability, some of which seem to have worked fairly well, like Citi Trends (CTRN), while others faltered after a few quarters of overperformance, like Children's Place (PLCE) or Torrid (CURV).

This is the main risk for Duluth today. The company is implementing a new strategy of discounting less and avoiding unprofitable (mostly online) sales. This strategy could play out perfectly from the get-go, with the company consistently improving its YoY profitability, or it may face setbacks.

The company could see its revenues decrease so much that even the gross margin gains are not enough. There are clearly other problems in assortment and design, and many customers have probably gotten used to discounting already (especially for a company that applied discounting so widely as Duluth).

As quarters build up, showing a consistent delivery of execution, then we can have more confidence that at least the internal factors are in the shareholders' favor. For the time being, we have one quarter only.

This is all in the context of a macroeconomic environment that is uncertain but where the bias tilts towards more challenges for consumer-facing companies.

Therefore, let us work with scenarios and see what is priced in.

If the company cannot improve permanently, then it generates no profitability at all, but losses, as it has done in the past two years. In that case, the value of the stock has to be very low, or even minimal, as an asset without profits is worth very little.

In an extremely bullish scenario, with the company returning to FY21 profitability, the name could generate $30 million in net income. This was peak Duluth, and believing it will be average Duluth going forward is a stretch of the mind, but for scenario purposes, let us consider it. Assuming a 10x earnings multiple on those profits, the name could be worth $300 million.

If both scenarios had the same probabilities, then we would have a 50% chance of $300 million and a 50% chance of $0 (if the company cannot generate profits, it is worth nothing over time). That is what the market is pricing today, approximately, at a market cap of $130 million.

I do not believe the chances of failure (current situation, sustained for several years, and driven by strategic factors) and the chances of impressive success (once in the company's history, during the best period for retail in the past twenty years) are the same. Therefore, I do not agree with this valuation.

If we go back to the 2015-2020 period, during which the company was under the guidance of the current CEO, then net profitability was closer to $18-22 million. If we use this scenario as the base, the probabilities between $0 and $200 million (P/E of 10x), for a market cap of $130 million, are about 70% for the 'historic average' scenario.

Still, I don't think a 70% chance of returning to that profitability is realistic yet. I believe the stock price has overreacted. Therefore, I maintain a Hold rating.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery: Don't Be Blindsided By The Paramount Skydance Bid
data.description:
The Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) stock had its best day on record, surging 29% on September 11 after the Wall Street Journal reported that rival Paramount Skydance (PSKY) is potentially preparing an offer to acquire the company. The development adds a potential sale to WBD’s list of options, which also includes an impending split planned for completion by April 2026.

In the following analysis, we’ll provide an overview of WBD’s upcoming options—i.e., a sale vs. a split—and discuss their potential implications on the stock’s price outlook.

Paramount Skydance Offer

Wall Street Journal reported earlier this week that Paramount Skydance is allegedly preparing an offer to acquire WBD in its entirety, which consists of both the Streaming & Studios, and legacy Global Linear Networks businesses. Although the news remains in speculation, with neither company providing confirmation, WBD investors have responded favourably. The stock surged 29% on September 11, with upside momentum persisting into market open on September 12.

There are two major considerations on this news—namely, 1) potential regulatory hurdles, and 2) pricing considerations.

The first thing that comes to mind is the potential regulatory hurdle that Paramount Skydance and WBD could face if the two pursue a merger. This accordingly raises timing uncertainties over the closing of such a deal, which inadvertently raises execution risks as well. This is consistent with the two-year back-and-forth between Skydance and Paramount before the two finalized their merger last month. The concern is further corroborated by the extended antitrust reviews observed prior to the completion of the Warner Media and Discovery merger in April 2022, despite limited overlap in the two’s operations.

Since Paramount Skydance and WBD’s corporate structure and business model face significant overlap, a proposed merger could face heightened regulatory scrutiny this time around. Specifically, both companies currently operate two of America’s largest linear networks and direct-to-consumer streaming and studio businesses. This includes Paramount+ and HBO Max, which collectively reach almost 4% of total TV screentime. Paramount Pictures and Warner Brothers also host some of the world’s most significant IPs, including Mission: Impossible and Harry Potter, respectively.

There have also been industry concerns over the misalignment of a potential Paramount Skydance and WBD merger with the current Trump administration’s interests, given President Trump’s personal disputes with both companies. For instance, President Trump has long loathed WBD’s CNN for broadcasting “fake news,” while also having launched a lawsuit against Paramount for a deceptive interview aired during the elections last year that ended in a $16 million settlement. This accordingly raises concerns over the durability of WBD’s premature valuation upsurge this week on news of a potential tie-up with Paramount Skydance.

The second consideration that comes to mind is WBD management’s intentions for the company’s future. Specifically, the company remains focused on reducing its debt load, while also optimizing its Streaming & Studios business’ growth and earnings accretion amid intensifying industry competition. This aligns with the company’s recently confirmed intentions to pursue a split of the company by April 2026, following its organizational restructure into Global Linear Networks and Streaming & Studios completed in June.

Current CEO David Zaslav has also reiterated his optimism that the split should unlock a significant premium for the fast-growing Streaming & Studios business, as the majority of WBD’s current debt load would be spun out with the Global Linear Networks unit. As a result, for WBD to entertain a merger proposal from Paramount Skydance, the offer would need to at least match the premium that Zaslav’s looking for.

Since Zaslav is looking to unlock incremental value for WBD shareholders post-split, we believe a sum-of-the-parts valuation exercise could reasonably gauge the bid price that he’d desire from Paramount Skydance.

Our forecast currently expects WBD’s consolidated adjusted EBITDA, less corporate and intersegment eliminations, to expand at a five-year CAGR of less than 1%. This reflects the drag observed primarily in the Global Linear Networks business, which remains in secular decline and poised to impact the scalability of profit margins.

Under the SOTP valuation approach, we’ve assessed the industry median NTM EV/EBITDA multiples observed across each of the streaming, studio, and linear TV network industries. The multiple is applied to our 2025, 2026, and 2027 adjusted EBITDA estimates for each of WBD’s current Streaming, Studios, and Global Linear Networks operations to determine their respective enterprise values. These enterprise values are then adjusted for their respective portion of net debt allocated based on their share of consolidated adjusted EBITDA to compute the estimated equity value. The net debt adjustment is consistent with management’s expectations that the majority of WBD’s current leverage will be spun out with the Global Linear Networks business post-split. Our exercise yields an estimated price target of $10, $10, and $9 per share for each of the Streaming, Studios, and Global Linear Networks businesses. Adjusted for intersegment eliminations of $4 per share, the SOTP valuation approach estimates $25 per share for WBD.

Warner Bros. and Discovery Global Split

The other option that’s been in the works for WBD since December 2025 is the breakup of the company. Following the completion of its restructuring in June, which splits the company into Streaming & Studios and Global Linear Networks, WBD has announced intentions for their respective spin-offs to be completed by April 2026. Specifically, the Streaming & Studios business, which includes Warner Bros. Television, Warner Bros. Motion Picture Group, DC Studios, HBO, HBO Max, and Warner Bros. Gaming Studios, as well as their respective IPs, will be branded under a new company called “Warner Bros.” led by Zaslav. Meanwhile, the Global Linear Networks business, which includes CNN, U.S. TNT Sports, Discovery, Discovery+, Bleacher Report, and other “free-to-air channels across Europe,” will be housed under a new company called “Discovery Global,” led by current WBD CFO Gunnar Wiedenfels.

Given the WBD stock has performed better on news of a potential sale of the company than on management’s earlier announcement of an impending split, it suggests markets have reservations about the company’s future prospects. We view this potential skepticism as reasonable, given WBD’s elevated execution risks, which are represented primarily by the significant debt burden at its declining linear TV operations, and intensifying competition facing its higher growth streaming and film business.

As a result, we believe a fair post-split price estimate for the stock would likely land somewhere between the optimal SOTP premium of $25 per share, as discussed in the earlier section, and WBD’s current pre-split intrinsic value estimated at $14 per share (discussed in the later section). This accordingly yields a post-split price target of $20 per share, representing upside of 14% based on the WBD stock’s September 12 open price of $17.92 per share. However, to sustainably unlock this premium, it’d not only require the completion of the upcoming WBD split, but also consistent delivery on both companies’ goals—i.e., deleveraging for Discovery Global and earnings accretion for Warner Bros.

The Status Quo Remains

Considering the regulatory and pricing hurdles facing WBD’s sale option, and execution risks facing the split option, we believe the stock remains at risk of gravitating back towards its status quo price of $14 per share.

The $14 per share price target is computed by applying the median EV/EBITDA multiple observed across WBD’s comparable peers, reflecting the market’s moderate risk appetite for blended media and entertainment businesses that consist of linear TV, streaming, and film operations.

Since WBD is still at least seven months away from a finalized split—and much longer if it’s contemplating a sale—investor attention is likely to eventually come back to the company’s existing execution hurdles. This includes WBD’s ability to build FCF and move towards its deleveraging target while gaining market share in its streaming growth driver against intensifying industry competition.

As a result, we believe WBD’s current situation will put its upcoming quarterly earnings updates back into focus, making them key near-term catalysts for the stock’s performance outlook. We also believe investors will remain more grounded in their expectations this time around, and possess greater conservatism over WBD’s execution of its impending split or sale. The incremental cautiousness would reflect adequate sentiment, given previous market expectations for a post-merger premium unlock in April 2022 that had failed to take off.

Final Thoughts

While the potential Paramount Skydance offer for WBD has reignited investor optimism for the stock, we remain incrementally cautious of the recent upsurge’s durability. Although the SOTP valuation for WBD is favourable, unlocking this premium would remain dependent on continued execution on its deleveraging and FCF accretion efforts, as well as a completed sale, which could face significant regulatory challenges. Meanwhile, attaining the potential post-split premium would also rely on consistent achievement of market share gains to sustain growth at the core Warner Bros. Streaming & Studios business—the key purpose of the company’s restructure and decision to reverse course on the April 2022 merger. However, this remains a significant challenge given intensifying market competition.

Taken together, with consideration of near-term execution risks amid WBD’s extended schedule from its upcoming split or sale, we remain cautious of a value reversion to its status quo price of $14 per share.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Double Down Before It Breaks Out Higher
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

My first investment research article on Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET) units came out in August 2023 with a "Buy" rating, and the total return level since then amounted to over 52% while the S&P 500 index (SP500, SPY) delivered only ~48% for the same period, so ET really stood out among energy plays in today's tech-dominant environment.

Unfortunately, my most recent article on ET, which was published in February 2025 (also with a "Buy" rating), caught the local top. Since then, the unit price declined by over 16%, so the total return of negative 11.59% is a stark difference compared to what SPY managed to show for the same period this time:

Despite this serious relative weakness of ET vs. SPY and virtually any other broader market index out there, I think it's time to double down on the unit and buy before the price starts to break out higher after a long consolidation phase. The business is doing exceptionally well - much better than what the current unit price reflects, in my opinion, and hence an obvious repricing is just a matter of time, given the upcoming earnings expansion over the next few years.

Why Do I Think So?

It might seem like the most recent Q2 2025 earnings print wasn't really remarkable, with ET's revenues of $19.24 billion falling by over 7% YoY and missing the consensus estimate by a hefty number of ~$3.29 billion (~14.59%), which was the heaviest miss in recent quarters.

But their adjusted EBITDA of $3.9 billion (+2.9% YoY) saved the day because a) it showed a positive YoY dynamic and b) it was well enough to bring the distributable cash flow to over $2 billion and cover the payouts. For Q2 in particular, ET's coverage ratio was ~1.7x, so it should have been read by income-seeking investors as a sign of strength, despite the revenue's YoY drop.

Anyway, the non-GAAP EPS of $0.32 missed the consensus by 1 cent, and the unit price kept its downward trajectory, as the market seemingly decided to focus on what it usually focuses on - the conventional headline figures.

The devil is in the details, as the saying goes, and it's important to see the broader picture of ET's strength through its business areas.

ET's Midstream segment, which saw its adjusted EBITDA climb to ~$768 million (+10.82% YoY) thanks to the increase in legacy Permian Basin volumes and the "successful integration of the WTG assets."

At the same time, their Interstate Natural Gas segment also posted a strong adjusted EBITDA rise from $392 million to $470 million in a single year (+20% YoY), thanks to "higher contracted volumes on several key pipeline systems," based on the commentary.

But the sales wouldn't decline that much on a YoY basis if there were no headwinds, and such came from the NGL and Refined Products segment, where ET lost $37 million in adj. EBITDA because of "lower gains from inventory optimization and weaker blending margins" (compared to the strong 2024). Also, the Intrastate transportation & storage segment saw a $44 million hit on the EBITDA level:

As we know, the "slower-than-initially-expected recovery in dry gas regions" led ET's management team to guide FY2025 adj. EBITDA to be at (or slightly below) the lower end of its $16.1-16.5 billion range, and it was another point of concern for those listening to the post-earnings call's commentary.

Anyway, the current headwinds seem to be only temporary, as the lower transportation revenues on their Bakken pipeline we saw in Q2 and the startup of the TMX pipeline expansion temporarily pulled Canadian barrels away from other routes, hence creating a vacuum that impacted Bakken flows. The CEO noted on the earnings call that as Canadian production growth fills this new capacity, those volumes should soon return to ET's system:

But we also look at how do we keep our pipelines full today, and more importantly, for the next 10 or 15 years. And what's going on in Canada is exciting to us. There's a lot more egress needs to get out. And what better way than to help Canadian producers get production out to a pipeline that already exists. No risk on building, no risk on any kind of other issues. And so we're excited about that.

We think it's a great opportunity to help Canadian producers. But more importantly, we're going to do everything we can to make sure the producers in North Dakota have an outlet on our pipeline. But in addition to that, we have the ability to increase by adding pumps, capacity or pipeline. And so it fits very well in working with Enbridge to help get more production out of the Canadian.

What I liked to see in Q2 was the fact that ET's deployed $2 billion in organic growth capital is already producing ~200 MMcf/d, and it's a great achievement from the team. Going forward, unitholders are going to see increased distributions because the business is growing well operationally, and the overall expansion is taking place in a profitable way. The company has already announced another distribution increase to $0.33/unit, pushing the forward yield to an attractive 7.60% for 2025. Interestingly, the consensus "dividend" estimates look pretty solid, with a 2.27% and 4.34% increase expected for 2026 and 2027 fiscal years, respectively, and the yield rising above 8% assuming no change in the unit price for the next 2 years:

The "value" factor ET holds for its unitholder to date is further enriched with the "growth" factor that comes from the AI trends of the past years. The management team only confirmed what I was describing and expecting in my previous takes on ET, noting that they've been in "advanced discussions" with numerous data centers and gas-fired power plants.

Now for a brief update around our new natural gas opportunities for new power plant and data center development. We continue to see a significant level of activity from demand pull customers to supply, store and transport natural gas for gas-fired power plants, data centers and industrial and onshore manufacturing. And we remain in advanced discussions with several facilities in close proximity to our footprint. We would expect these types of projects to generate revenue relatively quickly.

And what I think is fundamentally important for those wishing to hold ET units for the long term - Energy Transfer was initially set up in a way that maximizes their value proposition to DCs, and that's why, as long as the energy demand grows within the United States, the whole AI theme becomes a long-lasting catalyst for ET.

These data centers have come out of nowhere. It's a huge and enormously upside for companies like us that have big inch pipes all over the U.S. in very well-located areas for these types of projects, but they're different. And they're different in a couple of ways. One, these aren't a plant that cost $1 billion or $0.5 billion. These are $50 billion, $60 billion, $100 billion-type facilities that we're building to. So these things don't just happen overnight. It takes time. And even the announcement on the Desert Southwest, it took 3.5 years to develop that. So it has taken time. I, we are going to be more careful about what we say, but let's say this. I can't say this.

The management said they've already signed their 1st "significant deal with a behind-the-meter hyperscaler in Texas," a contract that has already grown from 80,000 Mcf/d to 380,000 Mcf/d with the potential for more. And again, it's just one hyperscaler, with likely more AI-related clientele to come shortly.

It became an "investment meme" when tech investors started to call Nvidia (NVDA) a "shovel seller" on this "AI Klondike", but what I think still few people on the market realize is that stable energy production is what's going to define the ultimate tech leader within the mega cap group. The firm that sells the option of a stable energy generation at sufficient capacities is going to be the real "shovel seller," and Energy Transfer seems to have all that might be needed for obtaining this status, in my understanding.

I've already mentioned the unit's distribution yield above, and it looks very tempting not just on the forward basis, but already to date. What I'd like to add here is that the unit itself trades at almost a decade-low valuation when it comes to the FWD EV/EBITDA ratio:

And at the same time, the debt-to-equity ratio is comfortably below 1, with EBITDA-to-interest staying well over 4 and being above the 10-year average since 2020, so I see no debt-related explanation for the multiple discount that ET trades for right now.

Assuming a 10% discount on the next year's earnings, and taking into account the forward distribution payout of $1.38 (per Seeking Alpha), I get a fair value per unit at $21.38 at a 3.5% distribution growth, according to a DDM model:

Add the 7.8%+ yield on that, and you'll get an upside potential of almost 30%. With all that said, I think ET is still looking too tempting and cheap to ignore.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

Just as I noted in my February take on ET, the firm is making progress with its balance sheet, but I think prospective investors should be aware of the rising interest expenses as the company may encounter debt service problems and distribution maintenance issues due to energy market declines or unforeseen operational hurdles.

Also, the "AI catalyst" is already well-known to a lot of people, and it just didn't help ET beat the market in the past few weeks. As long as the demand for the units doesn't arise, it's going to be hard for ET to show sufficient returns to its unitholders.

And again, I may be wrong in my valuation findings, because at a higher discount rate (at 11-15% or more) and a lower long-term distribution growth assumption, ET may be overvalued quite meaningfully:

The Bottom Line

I know the risks are there, but show me a riskless investment today - even what used to be taken as safe haven bonds tend not to behave the way they used to before.

Anyway, speaking about ET's opportunity, I think it's now clearer than ever. First, I see no operational cracks, and the declines the firm went through in the recent past seem to be temporary in nature. Second, the AI theme is real, and ET stands out as the go-to solution for hyperscalers in the next months thanks to its wide and well-built-out infrastructure. And third, the units are cheap, basically no matter what metric you choose to take for analysis.

I think when we see the first real rate cuts, the unit is going to break out to the upside. I'm not a technical analysis guru, but I know that the longer the consolidation phase, the higher the price move tends to be. And after over 3 months of correction, ET is forming into a clear flag pattern that can send it flying to the previous "sell zone" in a matter of days:

Energy Transfer is one of my favorite energy-related AI names out there, and I keep my "Buy" unchanged for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: QuantumScape's Volkswagen Breakthrough Offers Hope To Bulls As U.S. EV Momentum Stalls
data.description:
QuantumScape's (NYSE:QS) solid-state batteries hold the potential to jolt awake a moribund market for EVs in the U.S. after policy developments from Trump's drill, baby, drill mantra look set to reverse EV adoption momentum. The systematic rollback of EV emissions rules and purchase incentives has sparked a gasoline revival for the traditional automakers. Detroit is dusting off previously shelved CapEx plans for new gas-powered models or dropping targets to move to fully electric lineups. The landmark One Big Beautiful Bill (OBBBA) removed Biden's $7,500 EV purchase tax credit. It also ended fines for automakers who miss fuel-economy targets, mitigating the need to spend billions of dollars in regulatory credits. This had formed a critical incentive for automakers to ramp up the production of new EV models and had formed Tesla's (TSLA) most profitable business line. Hence, EVs will need to better compete with gas cars on technology, and solid-state batteries will be a key part of this competition.

However, QuantumScape at its current valuation reflects far too much euphoria in a market that could be set to be significantly more truncated on the back of the OBBBA. The international market also faces some headwinds with Chinese EVs, primarily built on CATL batteries, taking up an increasingly larger amount of EV sales. Chinese brands formed 5.1% of new vehicle registrations across 28 European countries for the first half of 2025, 2x the market share a year ago. To be clear, I see it as unlikely that these brands would use a QS battery. QuantumScape wants to replace conventional lithium-ion batteries with its non-combustible, anode-free lithium-metal batteries. These have materially enhanced energy density, meaning greater range for EVs, faster charging, and a safety profile that has eluded traditional combustible lithium-ion batteries. QS batteries would be faster charging than all of TSLA's current models, including those of Rivian (RIVN) and Porsche.

Valuation, Free Cash Flow, And Liquidity

QS is expensive. The company currently trades at a $5.6 billion market cap against fiscal 2025 second quarter revenue of zero; it's pre-revenue. Bulls have hinged their investment on the broader landscape for EVs in aggregate with perceived operational momentum and manufacturer engagement with its batteries. The company's roadmap places the launch of its first commercial product, the QSE-5, in 2026. This will have a less than 15-minute fast charge and an energy density of 844 Wh/L, beyond the density of current market options. I last covered QS in 2023, highlighting the potential of these batteries, but how commercial traction might have become an event ever beyond the horizon.

The first commercial product is via Volkswagen's (OTCPK:VLKAF) (OTCPK:VWAGY) PowerCo SE and could see QuantumScape's batteries used in the technology stack of a Ducati motorcycle. The launch of the product would represent a mini-inflection point, as it would form one of the first anode-free solid-state batteries to be used in a commercial real-world setting. Volkswagen premiered the future product at a September press conference in Munich. When the motorcycle will launch is still in the air, with downstream sales unlikely to be material when considered against QuantumScape's current market cap.

QS also places its batteries ahead of the competition, with the space an intensely crowded field of established companies like Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and startups like SES AI (SES) and Factorial Energy. The key metrics to monitor for QS in lieu of revenue will be free cash flow (FCF) and short-term liquidity. FCF came in at negative $70.1 million during the second quarter, with the company's cash and cash equivalents at $800 million. Liquidity has dipped by 15% over QuantumScape's year-ago comp and was down by $63 million sequentially.

Critically, QuantumScape's current liquidity position means a multi-year cash run rate at its current rate of burn. The company has stated that the current liquidity balance, in aggregate with additional payments being made by PowerCo through the expansion of its existing licensing agreement, should provide a cash runway far into 2029. The PowerCo team-up would see QS receive up to $131 million over the next two years.

Conclusion

QuantumScape's lofty valuation reflects the promise of solid-state batteries to materially advance the adoption of EVs, especially in a new reality where tax incentives have been stripped away and there is no regulatory stick to force the traditional automakers to pursue new EV models. In my opinion, QS currently sits purely in the realm of speculation, with a significant level of medium- to long-term operational momentum already baked into the current stock price. However, the company has built a formidable level of liquidity, hedging the need for selling new equity in the short term and providing a boost for bulls. QS is a hold.

data.title: Forget Enbridge: Here's Why TC Energy Is The Better Choice Today
data.description:
Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) and TC Energy (NYSE:TRP) are two leading Canadian midstream infrastructure giants. In addition to avoiding the K-1 tax form that many investors dread, these two companies offer investors a very attractive combination of yield, safety, and growth potential, as well as significant exposure to the AI boom, all in one package.

In this article, I will compare them side by side and share why I believe TRP currently gets the edge over ENB.

Enbridge: A Diversified Midstream Giant

When looking at them from a qualitative standpoint, ENB has considerable strengths. It is in many ways a utility, as less than 1% of its EBITDA is exposed to commodity prices, making it a very stable cash flow machine, with 80% of it inflation-protected, while over 98% of its EBITDA is either regulated or take-or-pay contracted with investment-grade counterparties. This means that its cash flow profile is extremely stable.

Additionally, ENB has a large, well-diversified business model that includes a very large liquids business, gas transmission business, gas distribution and storage business, and a growing renewable power business. All of these segments give it significant opportunities to invest in growth projects and attractive risk-adjusted returns. As a result, it expects to grow its EBITDA at between a 7-9% CAGR through 2026 and a 5% CAGR in the years following. Meanwhile, it expects to grow its distributable cash flow per share and earnings per share at a similar rate, indicating that it should be able to grow its intrinsic value per share and its dividend per share at a pace that beats inflation moving forward.

On top of its underlying cash flow strength and numerous opportunities to invest in growth projects, ENB also has a strong balance sheet with a BBB+ credit rating and a leverage profile that is declining due to its significant growth. Currently, the company has a 4.7x debt-to-EBITDA ratio, which lies right in the middle of its 4.5x to 5.0x debt-to-EBITDA target range. Meanwhile, as it continues to bring growth projects online with its huge $32 billion of secured capital projects at the end of the decade thus far, it will likely see its leverage ratio fall further due to increasing EBITDA.

The company's qualitative strength has driven very stable cash flow growth over time, which in turn has fueled significant dividend growth consistency, as ENB has now grown its dividend each year for 30 straight years, despite all sorts of turmoil in the energy markets and the economy over that period of time.

One distinctive aspect of ENB's business model that needs to be kept in mind, though, is that nearly 50% of its EBITDA comes from its liquids business. An additional 3% of its EBITDA, which pushes it to a full 50% of the business, is from its renewable power generation business, with the remaining 50% coming from its gas transmission, distribution, and storage businesses. This means it is far from a natural gas pure play and is more of a diversified midstream company between natural gas, liquids, and even has a little bit of renewable power exposure.

TC Energy: A Natural Gas Pure Play With AI Upside

Meanwhile, TRP also has exceptional quality in its business, as it has delivered 25 straight years of dividend growth, serving as evidence of its underlying fundamental strength.

Similar to ENB, TRP has a very defensively positioned business model with virtually all of it either contracted or regulated, and the vast majority of it regulated with investment-grade counterparties, making it utility-like in nature. In fact, 77% of its expected EBITDA is regulated, 20% is long-term take-or-pay contracted, and only 3% is exposed to commodity prices, making it very utility-like in nature, similar to how ENB is structured.

However, in contrast to ENB, which is more of a 50-50 mix between natural gas and oil-related businesses, TRP is 90% exposed to natural gas pipelines, 7% exposed to its Bruce Power business, and 3% invested in other power and energy solutions businesses, making it effectively a natural gas pure-play investment. This positions it very well for the high expected demand growth in natural gas, fueled in part by the AI boom moving forward.

In fact, TRP transports up to 30% of North American LNG feed gas demand, has over 165 direct connections to power generators across North America, and also has key connections to major LNG export facilities. It is also very well positioned to benefit from the data center build-out in the U.S., with approximately two-thirds of the 350-plus data centers being built in North America located within 50 miles of its assets, giving it a competitive advantage to win projects to provide natural gas to them as a power source.

Additionally, TRP's power and energy solutions portfolio has exposure to nuclear power, which is another segment that is expected to boom in the AI era. On top of that, its balance sheet has become much stronger recently as it has moved aggressively to deleverage, bringing its debt-to-EBITDA ratio to a very attractive level of around 4.7x, with an 18-year weighted average term to maturity on its debt, giving it very well-laddered debt maturities.

Its leverage ratio is very low compared to other utility businesses, while its cash flow profile is very similar to those businesses. Therefore, even though its leverage is on the high side relative to many of its midstream peers, it is still within its target range. It has a strong investment-grade credit rating, and when adjusting for the quality of underlying assets, especially the fact that they are natural gas-focused with strong growth tailwinds, TRP stands out as quite low risk from a qualitative standpoint.

Head-to-Head: Valuation and Growth

Turning to the valuation, both businesses are trading for very similar next 12-month enterprise value to EBITDA multiples, with TRP trading at 12.67x and ENB trading at 12.63x. ENB does have a slight edge in the dividend yield department with a 5.8% next 12-month dividend compared to TRP's 4.9% next 12-month dividend yield. Moreover, both are likely to grow their dividends at a similar 3-5% annualized dividend per share CAGR moving forward based on recent management performance and commentary.

However, TRP looks poised to generate significantly stronger EBITDA growth moving forward, with it likely to deliver a high single-digit EBITDA CAGR, whereas ENB is likely going to deliver closer to a 5% EBITDA CAGR. The main reason for this is that TRP is effectively a natural gas pure play with some additional exposure to other high-growth power generation businesses like nuclear, whereas ENB has nearly half of its business focused on much slower-growing oil and refined products businesses. ENB also appears to be going a little bit out of its circle of competence with its renewable power business investments.

Key Risks to Keep in Mind

While both ENB and TRP are low risk due to their strong balance sheets and utility-like cash flows, the biggest risks facing them are operational and regulatory risks, as well as interest rate risk.

Operational and regulatory risks stem from the fact that they are both engaged in huge capital projects that involve a lot of construction and permitting, which can often run into snags and cost overruns. Additionally, they operate in multiple countries, in particular, TRP, which has material Mexican exposure that adds some geopolitical risk.

Their interest rate exposure comes from their fairly high leverage and their bond-like cash flows, which means that when long-term interest rates rise, their interest expense tends to go up, and the present value of their long-duration cash flows will often decline. As they get compared to bond yields, this means that their stock prices can be weighed down, at least in the near term, due to higher-for-longer interest rate exposure. That said, their underlying cash flows are largely inflation-protected, which helps insulate them from some of this risk.

Investor Takeaway

While both ENB and TRP are attractive for risk-averse investors who want a utility-like mid-single-digit yield with mid-single-digit annualized dividend per share growth, I give TRP the edge. With similar valuations, TRP has much better growth prospects given its natural gas-focused portfolio and strong positioning in AI-related demand growth. This is why, between the two, even though I rate both a hold and do not own either at the moment due to their relatively high valuations compared to some of their peers, the only one of the two that I have ever held - and the one I would be most likely to add back into my portfolios at High Yield Investor - is TRP.

data.title: Can You Retire On $500,000?
data.description:
Introduction:

First of all, we want to say that everyone should save and invest as much as possible and set retirement savings goals high enough, even if they may seem out of reach. If the goalpost is set high enough, the investor will likely strive and find new ways to reach those goals. All that said, such goals are highly individualistic, based on personal needs and circumstances, and cannot be generalized. For most middle-class folks, we would recommend setting a minimum goal of $1 million, but preferably $1.5 million. When it comes to retirement savings, often more is better.

So, what if you cannot save $1 million? You may be too late in starting a retirement account, or you did not save enough. Maybe you saved over the years, but circumstances forced you to withdraw from them, and now it is not nearly enough. Or maybe retirement always looked too distant in the future. Whatever the case may be, there is always hope, and one need not panic; all you need is the right perspective of the situation and proper planning for the future.

Let’s say you never saved much for retirement. You are already in your mid-50s, and you only have $100,000 in your retirement accounts. Also, your income is modest, and you think you can only work for another 10-12 years. In such a scenario, can you start now and save $500,000 by the time you retire at 65? Assuming you are starting from a nominal savings of $100,000, to get to $500,000, you probably need to save $20,000 to $25,000 each year, and the rest can come from compounding. More importantly, can $500,000 suffice for 30 years of retirement and afford you a middle-class living standard? Sure, it was not long ago when $500,000 was still considered a sizable amount to retire with a reasonably comfortable middle-class living. But the recent bout of inflation has changed much of that. Now, it may be very difficult to manage retirement with only $500,000; however, it is not impossible, and you have options. You may have to be innovative and be flexible enough in terms of where you retire and how you spend your money in retirement.

Let’s assume you saved $500,000 in your retirement accounts by the time you retire at 65. We will also assume that you own a home now worth $400,000 (acquired at a much lower price 15 years ago, prior to retirement), but you still have a mortgage balance of $150,000. So, you have equity in the house worth about $250,000. However, this amount is not your retirement capital, as you still need a place to live.

Here are the major decisions our retiree will have to make:

  1. The first major decision you will have to make is to move to a much cheaper place (even if you have to move out of state). You may even have to downsize, which will also help in reducing other living expenses like utility bills and property taxes. You should find a home that costs you less than the equity in your current house so that the new place is fully paid for with no mortgage to worry about. You should also choose a state where both the state taxes and property taxes are low.
  2. The second most important decision you would make is when to start drawing Social Security. Assuming our retiree couple is 65, we would recommend waiting until the full retirement age of 66 years and 10 months (or 67 to round it off). We will also recommend not withdrawing too much from your retirement capital for the intervening two years. Instead, our retiree should look for part-time work or an online gig to make up for the lack of social security income. Let’s assume your social security benefits (for the couple) at 67 are $1,800 per person, and for the couple is $3,600.
  3. At 65, you are eligible for Medicare benefits, so healthcare expenses will be reasonable. But, with Medicare premiums and supplementary insurance, let’s assume you will have an expense of $500 a month for the couple.
  4. We will also assume that in retirement, you keep only one car, which is fully paid. With ride-share options available these days, most folks (in retirement) can manage with one car.
  5. You choose to avoid having expensive hobbies. Let’s say you spend no more than $500 a month (average) on hobbies and travel.

Now, we can see that our retiree couple needs roughly $17,000 on a yearly basis from their investment capital or an income yield of 3.5% from their investments. To account for some unexpected exigencies, we will assume that they should shoot for a 4% yield or $20,000 income on an annual basis. If they can save something out of this amount, they can always reinvest.

Also, a point to note here. For the first two years, since they are delaying the social security payments until 67, they should work part-time and fill the income gap. However, there may be a need for additional income during these two years, thereby requiring a higher yield (just the first two years).

Investment Portfolio for Retirement with $500,000:

Here we will describe in detail and recommend a portfolio that should generate between 5% and 6% income and nearly market-matching growth over a long-term basis. Sure, if you only need 4% income, as in our above example, you can reinvest the excess of income (which may be a good problem to have).

We will suggest two buckets inside your investment portfolio. Also, to keep things simple enough, we will go for a fund-based buy-and-hold portfolio.

This will be an income-focused bucket that will also provide roughly 7% income.
