This portfolio generated more income than what our retiree couple needed, so they could reinvest the excess and grow the portfolio.

Risks with the Strategy:

As is true with any investment strategy, there are certain risks to the portfolio strategy suggested in this article. The biggest risk with this buy-and-hold two-bucket system is the market risk. If the overall market were to enter into a correction, the portfolio would also have losses. However, more than likely, the income should be protected. Moreover, there is “sequencing risk,” meaning what if the market were to take a deep dive just after you invest? The negative impacts of sequencing risk are potentially highest around the time of retirement. To mitigate both the above risks, one should invest in this strategy in many small installments rather than one lump sum. One can also utilize the dollar-cost-average method to invest in a new strategy, preferably 2-3 years (or more) prior to retirement.

Concluding Thoughts:

We are not suggesting that $500,000 is an appropriate amount or enough savings for everyone. In fact, one should aim for much higher. That being said, one size does not fit all. There are folks for whom it may still be a decent amount that, if properly invested, can serve them meaningfully. The portfolio described in the article can provide enough income along with Social Security, which should suffice for a middle-class living in many parts of the U.S. We have provided a hypothetical case study with a potential portfolio with $500,000 capital that can provide enough income and market-matched capital growth over the long term, and all that with a relatively low risk.

data.title: Vertex Pharmaceuticals: Buying The CF Fortress For The Journavx Optionality
data.description:
Introduction

My last look at Vertex Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:VRTX) followed JOURNAVX’s FDA approval for acute pain. While the company has done a splendid job extending the lifecycle of its multi-billion-dollar cystic fibrosis (CF) portfolio, it has been actively seeking blockbuster opportunities outside of the CF market. However, JOURNAVX has failed to live up to lofty expectations (e.g., $11 billion in peak sales estimates by some) after mixed clinical data.

However, given the strength of its CF franchise, pipeline optionality, and a seemingly conservative valuation per my DCF model, I recommended keeping VRTX a Buy but with tempered expectations for JOURNAVX.

The following article revisits Vertex in light of recent developments.

Recent Developments

VRTX has underperformed the market since my last evaluation, with two notable drops in May and August.

The first drop was associated with its first quarter 2025 report, in which it missed on GAAP EPS ($4.06 missed by $0.23) and revenue ($2.77 billion missed by $90 million). Vertex also adjusted its FY 2025 revenue outlook from “$11.75 billion to $12 billion” to “$11.85 billion to $12 billion.” What was most interesting, to me, about the report is that Vertex’s SG&A expenses are outpacing (YoY) its revenue growth. This was by the tune of 15.67% versus 2.59%, respectively, in Q1. This is, undoubtedly, associated with the relatively more challenging launches of CASGEVY (a gene therapy product) and JOURNAVX. This disparity eased a bit in the second quarter (+14.08% YoY SG&A vs. +11.28% YoY revenues). However, Vertex “piggybacked” its Q2 results with disappointing Phase 2 VX-993 data in treating acute pain after bunionectomy surgery, causing the August stock drop.

Like JOURNAVX (oral), 993 was another selective NaV1.8 blocker. Its intended role was to widen the franchise, especially as 993 is intravenous and, therefore, intended for higher exposures and hospital use. Following the Phase 2 failure, Vertex stopped development of 993 as a monotherapy for acute pain.

So, the focus remains on JOURNAVX. Its commercialization in moderate-to-severe acute pain and its clinical efforts in Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy, or DPN—a multi-billion-dollar market with blockbusters like LYRICA boasting share.

On the commercialization front, JOURNAVX generated $12 million in Q2. This represented its first full quarter after a March launch. The company noted that over 110,000 scripts were filled in its first four months. So far, so good, but in baseball terms, Vertex has merely retired one batter.

In the absence of long-term data and further study, treatment recommendations are inconclusive on JOURNAVX. I think payers are more interested in outcomes like opioid consumption and quality of recovery than in pain alone—especially given the abundance of cheap and generic pain drugs on the market.

“Pain” is a much different market than CF. There are endless models (post-surgery, chronic), descriptors (e.g., tingling, aching), causes (e.g., neuropathic), etc., of pain. It is also subjective. So, JOURNAVX needs to accumulate several trials in different pains to build its case for broad adoption. This is why its failure in painful lumbosacral radiculopathy (LSR)—a chronic disorder—was “painful” to VRTX. It also doesn’t bode well for other chronic pain disorders like DPN.

With that said, JOURNAVX’s broad label (approved for any types of moderate-to-severe acute pain in both hospital and outpatient settings) and safety advantages over opioids give it some runway. However, for JOURNAVX to become the $11 billion per annum “difference-maker” that the market once hoped for, it needs to be successful in many different models of pain. With this in mind, DNP results are critical (Phase 3 is underway).

Taking a closer look at its Q2 financial results, Vertex’s CF franchise continues to make for a solid and profitable floor for its valuation. Vertex’s $2.96 billion in Q2 revenue (up 12% YoY and beating expectations of ~$2.91 billion) was thanks in large part to its CF portfolio (its flagship therapy, TRIKAFTA, alone generated $2.55 billion in Q2). ALYFTREK (“triple combination therapy for CF”) is Vertex’s next-gen CFTR modulator, and it is off to a strong start with $156.8 million in Q2 sales. Moving forward, the anticipation is that Vertex will continue to grow the number of CF patients on its CF medicines as it gains approval for younger ages, secures reimbursements worldwide, and as CF patients live longer on therapy. Moreover, the innovative nature of these CF medicines grants the company high gross margins (~86%) and operating margins (~46%).

CASGEVY’s launch continues to drag. The company noted that just 16 patients were infused with CASGEVY in Q2 (over 30 cumulatively so far). Vertex remains active in securing authorized treatment centers, which are necessary for infusion. It is also working on expanding manufacturing and improving pre-treatment conditioning, which limits patient appeal as it is complex and exhaustive.

Turning to pipeline developments, Vertex is advancing a functional cure—zimislecel—for Type 1 diabetes. This is a very speculative pursuit, and others are also evaluating cell therapies for a T1D cure. Elsewhere, Vertex’s antibody drug povetacicept (from the Alpine acquisition) is in a pivotal Phase 3 trial in adults with immunoglobulin A nephropathy, or IgAN if you’d like to avoid twisting your tongue. But, again, this is not a low-hanging fruit of an indication. The IgAN treatment landscape is rapidly evolving, with many companies pursuing therapeutics and some new ones just recently approved (e.g., TARPEYO, FLISPARI). It is a really competitive space. Of course, Vertex’s pipeline is far more complex than my description, but I detailed what garners most of the market’s attention. Obviously, another breakthrough beyond CF and pain would be optimal, but there’s no clear and convincing evidence that one is on the horizon. Subsequently, Vertex remains very much attached to its CF franchise and JOURNAVX—for better or worse.

Valuation Assumptions

Of course, none of this means anything without the proper context – valuation.

Assumptions include Year 1 (FY 2025) revenue of $11.925 billion (midpoint of guidance), revenue growth of 8% YoY, FCF margin of 30% (based on TTM; assumes operational efficiency over time balances out with new drug launches), and a blended beta of 0.8. The rest is copy-and-paste (e.g., cash & equivalents, risk-free rate, shares outstanding).

On a cash flow basis, Vertex appears to be priced for ~8% YoY revenue growth and ~30% FCF margins, which appears appropriate.

Vertex probably doesn’t have much leeway when it comes to its margins. You’d expect the margins to actually compress a bit now that the company is diversifying beyond the “old and reliable” CF franchise. So, when I look at the sensitivity analysis that follows, I’m primarily focused on how changes to growth assumptions impact valuation.

Indeed, Vertex’s ability to outperform the market appears to rely very much on JOURNAVX. I think slow, steady, and sustained CF growth is pretty predictable and priced into the stock accordingly.

There are some scenarios that come to mind:

So, the current expectation appears to be that Vertex’s growth will soon moderate into the mid-to-high single digits—consistent with current consensus revenue estimates. This likely assumes low-to-moderate market success from JOURNAVX. It's hard to argue against this premise.

The Bottom Line

To wrap up, not much has changed with Vertex since my last look. It continues to enjoy a de facto monopoly in CFTR modulator treatments with some optionality in JOURNAVX. The latter is off to a solid start on the pain market, but the journey to becoming a multi-billion-dollar “franchise” is still a ways off and requires a lot more positive data. Meanwhile, Vertex’s pipeline is interesting, but they are pursuing very crowded R&D spaces. So, it’ll be interesting to see how it differentiates its drugs from competitors’. Moving forward, JOURNAVX’s commercial launch and pivotal DNP data will be critical catalysts. Looking ahead to its FQ3 2025 report (expected in early November), I would pre-commit to the following revenue bands as a means to avoid “dizzy” decisions following the topline numbers:

  • Bear band → <$18 million (uptake slower than expected; access; stocking or prescriber conversion lagging); trim position

  • Base band → $22-30M (healthy q/q ramp consistent with growing coverage and hospital adds); no sizing changes

  • Bull band → $35-50M (broad early adoption; GTN and stocking not impeding); add-on dips.

  • Blowout band → >$50M (step-function demand/rapid formulary penetration; signals durable franchise); add.

Until then, Vertex remains a Buy on its CF moat and optionality in JOURNAVX, pipeline assets, and ~$12 billion in cash/investments with no debt.

data.title: Getty Realty: Less Than 12x Earnings, 6.5% Yield, Raised Guidance. What More Can You Ask For?
data.description:
Introduction

The REIT (XLRE) sector as a whole still presents some attractive buying opportunities, although much less than roughly 3 years ago when the sector was in a bear market.

In 2022, I loaded up on REITs like Agree Realty (ADC) & VICI Properties (VICI) and have enjoyed some strong price appreciation despite the sector underperforming the overall market.

Moreover, with interest rates expected to decline this month and more rate cuts likely to follow following the latest jobs report, REITs are likely to enjoy some strong price appreciation over the next 12 to 24 months.

Getty Realty Corp. (NYSE:GTY), despite their strong fundamentals and performances, currently presents long-term investors with a great buying opportunity, trading less than 12x earnings on a forward basis. Furthermore, investors collect more than a 6% dividend yield while they wait.

In this article I discuss Getty Realty Corp.'s latest earnings, fundamentals, and why long-term income investors should consider this cheap REIT and collect over a 6% dividend yield while they wait.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Getty Realty Corp. back in April in an article titled: Could See Selling Pressure Ahead, But 6% Yield Is Attractive.

Due to the potential for economic weakening and troubled tenants with their keen focus on car washes, I suspected GTY could face selling pressure over the short-term.

However, their long-term fundamentals remained intact as a result of their strong occupancy, well-laddered debt, and upside potential to their price target of $33.43.

Since, the stock has enjoyed some upside, up 3.48% over the past 5 months. However, GTY has significantly underperformed the S&P (SP500), which is up 31.55% over the same period.

Solid Earnings

Getty Realty Corp reported their Q2 earnings at the end of July and delivered a solid report despite the miss on their bottom line. FFO missed analysts' estimates by $0.07, amounting to $0.49. But revenue of $53.26 million managed to beat by a solid $1.67 million, a growth rate of 6.65% year-over-year.

Although FFO declined from $0.56 in Q1 and from $0.55 year-over-year, the metric that matters the most, AFFO actually increased a penny from the previous year's quarter. AFFO of $0.59 was flat from Q1, and through the first 2 quarters amounted to $1.19, up 3.5% on an annualized basis.

In total, AFFO growth was higher through the first half of the year at 6.6%, amounting to $67.8 million. And this was likely due to the REIT's solid investment activity, investing $95.5 million through 2 quarters at a weighted-average cash yield of 8.1%.

Management saw higher yields, up from 7.9% in Q2'24 despite investment activity being lower than last year's $103.8 million, likely the result of a slowing economy. Management also stated they had an additional $90 million in investments under contract expected to close in the next 6 to 9 months.

Raised Guidance Amidst Uncertainty

During my last article, I touched on one of Getty's tenants, Zips Car Wash, filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy. Since then, the REIT has worked to successfully reposition the Zips portfolio.

GTY had a total of 12 properties leased to Zips, and during the quarter managed to re-lease 5 to two new car wash operators. The remaining properties are expected to be sold by year's end.

And due to the successful repositioning, management increased full-year AFFO guidance by $0.02 on the lower end, with AFFO now expected in the range of $2.40 - $2.41.

This represents a growth rate of 2.7% using the midpoint. Moreover, this safely covers their annual dividend of $1.89 with a conservative payout ratio of 78.6%. And with expected lower rates, the coming quarters could see management increase guidance further, resulting in potential upside.

Portfolio Concentration Could Be A Risk

Because Getty Realty Corp focuses on car washes, convenience stores, and quick-service restaurants, this is likely the reason the stock trades well-below the peer average.

And rightfully so as the tenant profile in their portfolio is a risk when compared to REITs like Agree Realty, who focuses on investment-rated tenants like Walmart (WMT), Home Depot (HD), and Auto Zone (AZO).

At less than 12x, GTY sits well-below peers like ADC, and even Realty Income (O), both of whom have forward multiples of 16.72x and 13.96x, respectively. But what investors should know is that GTY's management doesn't just focus on any properties, but their locations are strategic in nature.

These are also in high density areas and have nationwide locations. During the quarter, GTY invested $66.1 million in 9 quick-service restaurants, 6 auto service centers, 5 convenience stores, and 4 car washes. They also provided $4 million in funding for the construction of 3 auto service centers and 1 tunnel car wash.

Because Getty's annualized base rents have a good portion of its income coming from car washes at 20.5%, they are likely to see weakness if the U.S. economy manages to see a recession. And while the recent jobs report could be an indication of economic weakening, I think GTY will navigate headwinds successfully.

But going forward, investors should keep a close eye on further headwinds with potential bankruptcies and a decline in occupancy. But Getty Realty has maintained a strong occupancy so far, with 99.7% and 99.9% of rent collected, the same year-over-year.

Well-Laddered Balance Sheet

Another attractive metric is Getty Realty Corp.'s strong balance sheet, evident by their well-laddered debt and leverage ratio of 5.2x, 4.6x including their unsettled forward equity.

The REIT has no debt until 2028, putting them in a flexible position to see continued strong investment activity. Moreover, their liquidity position was strong at $400 million.

Upside Potential & Conclusion

While significant upside could be limited over the near-term, especially if we see further economic weakening in the coming quarters, GTY trading at less than 12x earnings, 6.50% yield, and strong occupancy is attractive for long-term investors.

Moreover, although we're expected to see rates decline next week, tailwinds could be priced in already for the REIT sector. But GTY should see upside from multiple rate cuts, likely over the next few months. If so, I see solid upside.

So, I happen to be in agreement with Wall Street and continue to rate them a buy despite the potential for weakness and limited upside in the coming months. If you're an income-focused investor looking for value in a frothy market, consider Getty Realty Corp.

data.title: 3 Dividend Stocks I'm Buying As Irrational Exuberance Takes Over
data.description:
Some people collect rare stamps, comic books, coins, or baseball cards. (Or, these days, hideous Labubu dolls.)

Me? I collect charts.

Every week, I show off the latest additions to my chart collection in these articles in an effort to understand and explain the economy, markets, and dividend investing. On top of that, for what it's worth, I also reveal and discuss my buy list. I don't expect (or want) anybody to follow my buys religiously, but I do hope to be a good source of ideas for fellow dividend growth investors.

Now that the sales pitch is out of the way ("what do I need to do to get you in a brand new Mazda today?"), let's see those charts.

Here's the agenda this week:

  • An attempt to explain the weird and conflicting inflation data.
  • Some compelling signals that irrational exuberance has taken hold in both the stock market and parts of the economy.
  • More conflicting data about the trajectory of the economy, wherein the labor market points to weakness but affluent consumer spending signals resilience.
  • Some comments on my slimmed down buy list.

Onward!

(In/De/Disin)Flation

Inflation readings have been weird lately.

For August, the producer price index unexpectedly declined, whereas the consumer price index rose more than expected.

Perhaps this indicates that on the business-to-business side of the economy, companies are "eating the tariffs" and allowing margin compression for the time being, whereas on the business-to-consumer side of the economy, companies are hiking prices whenever they are able to.

There's some evidence for this. For example, this helpful chart shows the percentage of items within the CPI that exhibited at least 3% price increases by month:

As you can see, the number of consumer items seeing significant price increases has jumped to nearly 60% of the total, the highest since the Fall of 2022.

Jeremy Schwartz of WisdomTree compiles a "real-time inflation" metric every month, substituting the lagged BLS housing data for market rent rates from the private sector. Back in 2021-2022, real-time inflation was significantly higher than the CPI reported, but from 2023 through the present, real-time inflation has been lower than the CPI reports.

This aligns almost perfectly with data from another private consumer price aggregator, Truflation, which currently (as of this writing) shows almost exactly the same level of inflation.

While non-lagged inflation is (and has been for years) lower than the reported number, it's notable that all inflation readings are heading higher. That is with residential rent rates basically flat in the face of a huge amount of new multifamily supply.

Used cars & trucks, tobacco & smoking products, and alcoholic beverages (all heavily exposed to imports and therefore tariffs) saw sharp price increases in August.

A lot of crosscurrents are intersecting right now to cast the inflation picture into disarray. A financially weak paycheck-to-paycheck (low- to moderate-income) consumer, a resilient affluent consumer, ample corporate margin buffer to temporarily absorb tariffs, and uncertainty about the enduring legality of across-the-board tariffs are keeping the inflation outlook uncertain.

The only certainty is that if tariffs remain in place indefinitely, their cost will be borne mostly by American businesses and consumers.

Irrational Exuberance

Irrational exuberance is hard to measure or define, but you know it when you see it.

It isn't just about asset valuations. It's a broad, culture-wide attitude toward risk. It is when a society seems to forget that bad things can happen and instead becomes consumed by the fear of missing out. It is the sense that others out there are getting rich quick, and I need to get rich quick as well. It is the sense that if I don't participate in the momentum right now, I will miss out and forever regret it.

Given the incredible and market-beating rallies in silver (SLV), gold (GLD), and Bitcoin this year, it is striking to find this exuberance in both traditionally "risk-on" assets and "risk-off" assets.

The S&P 500 (SPY) has returned a cool 12%+ this year, which is certainly respectable. Stocks are participating in the exuberance, to be sure. But the most exciting action is arguably happening outside the stock market, including in precious metals and crypto.

Bitcoin's rally appears to be driven primarily by a risk-on attitude, whereas gold and silver are probably driven by a combination of high fiscal deficits, geopolitical friction, a weakening US dollar, and the perception among many that stocks are expensive.

But the non-stock exuberance doesn't end there. No, no! We haven't even gotten to the most exciting stuff.

Like Pokemon cards, which are trouncing the SPY and even beating many AI-related stocks like Meta Platforms (META) this year.

The last time Pokemon cards surged in price like this was in 2020 and early 2021, during the peak of the bout of irrational exuberance. META also reached a peak during this period. Baseball card prices peaked a bit later, around the beginning of 2022.

Even the current Labubu doll craze seems reminiscent of the late '90s Beanie Baby bubble. Some have pointed out the striking similarities between the two phenomena. Collectors are frenetically accumulating dolls and driving up resale prices.

In 1998, when I was 7 years old, I went to the MLB All-Star baseball game in Denver, where everyone received a special edition Beanie Baby named "Glory." Outside the stadium after the game, people ran around, offering to buy these Beanie Babies for $300, $400, $500 or more. My dad and brother sold theirs, but I refused to part with mine.

("Let your winners ride" was my 7-year-old self's motto, you might say.)

Hopefully it isn't lost on you, dear reader, that the Beanie Baby bubble coincided with the Dot Com bubble of the late 1990s. On the surface, the two appear unrelated, but one could argue that they were both part of the broader zeitgeist of exuberance.

If the mad frenzy for precious metals, or for crypto, or for Pokemon cards, or for Labubu dolls was happening in isolation, it might simply be an interesting phenomenon. But when all are happening simultaneously, it strikes me as strong evidence that irrational exuberance has taken hold.

Now add to that high stock valuations.

The SPY's forward P/E ratio of over 22x has only been this high (in the modern era) in 2021 and in the late 1990s.

While the late '90s were clearly recognized as a bubble, both periods could accurately be characterized as being in the grips of irrational exuberance.

Notice in the chart below on the right that it isn't only the largest 10 stocks trading at rich valuations. The 490 stocks below them in the SPY are also trading at around the same level as their peak valuations in the late 1990s.

Then again, you'll notice that today, unlike the period from 1996 to 2000, the market cap and earnings of the largest US companies are growing roughly in line with each other. Back in the Dot Com bubble era, the market cap of Internet-related tech stocks surged first, and only in the following years (2001-2002) did the largest companies' earnings follow suit.

It isn't that every detail of today's tech-driven market is the same as it was during the Dot Com bubble. It's that the level of investor euphoria, risk appetite, and irrational exuberance today is reminiscent of the way it was back then.

It's the zeitgeist. It's in the social water we drink and the social air we breathe.

This doesn't necessarily mean the market is going to crash tomorrow, or next month, or perhaps ever. It's just a mere observation.

We are living in a period of irrational exuberance.

Economic Update: Weak Labor Market, Resilient Consumer

Paradoxically, the aforementioned irrational exuberance currently coexists with a rather mediocre and stagnant economy.

Fueled by flat to negative net immigration, AI-driven productivity increases, and the end of the post-COVID stimulus boom, growth in total US jobs has slowed to under 1%.

Historically, job growth slowing to less than 1% has been a pretty reliable indicator of an oncoming recession, but in the past, pre-recession slowdowns in job growth have been sharper than the gradual and grinding slowdown over the past few years.

For what it's worth, sentiment among those in the labor force today has sharply soured in recent months. Workers believe that getting a new job in a reasonable period of time would be very difficult today, and expectations of layoffs have been creeping higher over the last few years.

Sentiment about one's ability to obtain another job within 3 months if laid off has collapsed to its lowest level since the Great Financial Crisis. Is that just the fear of AI? Or does it communicate something about the economy more broadly?

Well, it is notable that after the brief temp worker boom in 2021 and early 2022, the number of temporary workers has slid rapidly.

Back in normal times, such huge and rapid declines in the temp workforce would reliably signal a weakening economy and an oncoming recession. Is it different this time?

Well, one thing is definitely different: The temp workforce has rapidly declined for three consecutive years without an officially declared recession. The economy continues to hum along just fine with fewer and fewer temp workers.

My guess is that AI will add fuel to this fire as employers find ways to make their hired staff more productive rather than utilize temp workers.

But while the labor market looks rather grim, other parts of the economy appear much more cheery.

For example, retail sales continue to grow.

Although real (inflation-adjusted) retail sales growth is rather anemic at less than 1%, it is still positive, which is better than most of the 2022-2023 period could say.

Affluent consumers continue to retain their jobs and continue to spend money. And the most recent wage growth data from Bank of America indicates that after a multi-year hiatus after the pandemic, higher-income households once again enjoy accelerating wage growth.

In the years following COVID, when the US suffered a severe labor shortage, lower-income households enjoyed rapid wage growth. Today, however, lower-income workers are seeing smaller and smaller raises, while higher-income workers are earning more and more — perhaps as a result of productivity gains.

The K-shaped economy reigns supreme.

This matters because affluent consumers account for most of the country's consumption. As of 2023, the top 40% of earners drove 60% of total consumer spending.

More recent data from Moody's Analytics suggests that the top 10% of earning households do roughly half of all consumer spending in the US.

If the affluent are doing well, then the US economy should also be doing well.

Here's one more positive sign: growth in bank lending is on the rise.

Bank lending growth isn't necessarily a leading indicator of recession, though. Prior to the 1990-1991 and 2001 recessions, growth in bank lending did slide considerably. But in the 2008-2009 recession — the worst since the Great Depression — bank lending didn't take a hit until well into the recession.

In any case, rising growth in bank lending probably can't be interpreted as a negative for the economy.

My Buy List

How then shall we invest amid all these confusing signals?

In a word: cautiously.

"Be fearful when others are greedy," as the Oracle of Omaha once quipped.

Your humble scribe has chosen the dividend growth investor's life, and my buy list reflects that. Assuming prices remain about where they are today, here's where I plan to devote any investable cash over this week:

Stocks Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.5% Mid-Single-Digit
American Tower (AMT) 3.5% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
H2O America (HTO) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
ETFs
iShares Ultra Short Duration Bond ETF (ICSH) 4.5% N/A
SPDR Utilities ETF (XLU) 2.6% Mid- to High-Single-Digit

Since I discussed AMT and XLU last week and have nothing new to add about HTO, I'll focus my comments elsewhere.

AMH is the only vertically integrated single-family rental REIT, meaning that it operates an internal build-to-rent development platform alongside its massive, largely Sunbelt and Midwestern single-family rental portfolio.

A weakening labor market isn't great for AMH, but the good news is that demographics and home buying affordability are still very favorable to SFR landlords like AMH.

Moreover, AMH looks reasonably valued. Its dividend yield of 3.5% is the highest it's ever been, its AFFO multiple of ~20x is around its lowest level in the last five years, and its price to operating cash flow is about 10% below its long-term average.

A very large cohort of Millennials are entering their prime single-family living years of life without the ability to afford buying a single-family home. That will undoubtedly channel a lot of that demand into the SFR market.

A near-future recession would certainly justify the price decline in AMH, to be sure. But (1) a near-future recession is far from guaranteed, and (2) AMH's long-term outlook remains strong, in my view.

Back to Partial Cash-Raising Mode

Given the above discussions about irrational exuberance and a weakening labor market, I deem it prudent to return to incremental cash-raising. My favorite vehicle to act as a cash equivalent is ICSH, both because of its low expense ratio of 8 basis points and its low-volatility.

Notice that the downside ICSH experienced during the rate-hiking cycle of 2022 was fairly minimal relative to peers like JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST), PGIM Ultra Short Bond ETF (PULS), and Vanguard Ultra-Short Bond ETF (VUSB):

For these reasons, I view ICSH as arguably the best place to stash cash.

While I'm not predicting it, if there is a significant correction in the stock market anytime soon, I want to have more dry powder than I presently do to take advantage of it.
